lessons.title: std::print and std::println: Type-Safe Output
lessons.motivation: Before C++23, programmers faced two uncomfortable extremes: fast and concise printf — but unsafe (a mismatched format specifier causes undefined behavior), or verbose std::cout with chained << operators — type-safe but slow to compile and incompatible with format strings. C++20 brought std::format, combining type safety with a convenient format-string syntax, but it returned an intermediate std::string that still had to be passed to std::cout. std::print in C++23 closes that gap: it is std::format plus direct output, with no unnecessary allocations.
lessons.theory:
std::print and std::println are declared in the <print> header and share the same format-string syntax as std::format: {}, {:>10}, {:.2f}, and other specifiers work unchanged. std::print(fmt, args...) writes the formatted string to stdout without a trailing newline; std::println(fmt, args...) appends \n at the end. Both functions have an overload whose first parameter is FILE*: std::println(stderr, "err: {}", msg) directs output to the standard error stream or any other open FILE*.

Why did std::print not arrive alongside std::format in C++20? The reason is encoding on Windows. printf on that platform does not transcode UTF-8 to the UTF-16 expected by the console API, so non-ASCII characters — Cyrillic, emoji, accented letters — come out garbled without additional code-page gymnastics. The committee chose to wait for a standardized solution: std::print is required to correctly transcode Unicode when writing to a console on all platforms.

Under the hood, std::print relies on two low-level helpers: std::vprint_unicode — for streams where Unicode correctness matters (consoles, terminals) — and std::vprint_nonunicode — for binary or plain-text streams where transcoding is unnecessary. Most users never need them directly: std::print and std::println select the right variant automatically.

The practical migration rule is straightforward: replace printf("x = %d\n", x) with std::println("x = {}", x), and fprintf(stderr, "err: %s\n", msg) with std::println(stderr, "err: {}", msg). No manual type-based format control, no unsafe specifiers — the compiler checks argument types at compile time.

examples.title: Basic std::print and std::println usage
examples.description: Printing a string, an integer, and a floating-point value using various format specifiers.
examples.expectedOutput:
Name: Alice
Age:    30
Score: 98.5
No newline here — same line

examples.title: Alignment, zero-padding, and alternate bases
examples.description: Aligning text into columns, printing numbers with leading zeros, and displaying values in hexadecimal and octal — all through the unified format-string syntax.
examples.expectedOutput:
left            right   center  
Item 01:     3.14
Item 02:     6.28
Item 03:     9.42
Dec: 255  Hex: FF  Oct: 377

lessons.title: std::format for Ranges and Custom Types
lessons.motivation: std::format arrived in C++20 and was immediately more ergonomic than printf and std::cout. But one frustrating gap remained: trying to format a std::vector<int> or a std::map<std::string, int> produced a compile error — standard containers were not supported out of the box. Similarly, your own types such as Point, Color, or Matrix had to be manually converted to a string before being passed to std::format. C++23 closes both gaps: built-in formatting support for any range, and an official hook for custom types through std::formatter<T> specialization.
lessons.theory:
In C++23 any range whose elements are formattable automatically becomes formattable itself. std::println("{}", vec) — where vec is std::vector<int> — prints [1, 2, 3]. This works for any type that satisfies the std::ranges::range concept, including arrays, lists, sets, and spans. The default format uses an opening bracket, elements separated by commas, and a closing bracket.

For finer control, ranges support special modifiers inside the format specifier. The n flag removes the brackets: {:n} produces 1, 2, 3 instead of [1, 2, 3]. The m flag enables "map style" for ranges of pairs: {"a": 1, "b": 2} — exactly the output you get when formatting a std::map. The s flag interprets a range of characters as a string. After a double colon (::) you can specify a per-element format: {::>4} right-aligns each element in a field of width 4.

To make your own type formattable, specialize the template struct std::formatter<T>. The specialization must provide two methods. The parse method receives a std::format_parse_context and reads the format specifier — the part of the format string between the : character and the closing }. If you need no custom specifiers, simply return ctx.begin() without any parsing. The format method receives a const reference to the object being formatted and a std::format_context, and writes its output through the helper function std::format_to(ctx.out(), ...). Once the specialization is defined, the type works in every formatting context: std::format, std::print, std::println, and as an argument inside more complex format strings.

When you need a configurable format rather than a fixed one, parse can read characters from the specifier and set flags on the formatter object itself. For example, an x flag could switch a color type's output from rgb(255,0,0) to #FF0000. The formatter's fields are initialized during each parse call and are accessible in format through this.

Beyond std::format_to, there is std::format_to_n(out, n, fmt, args...) — it writes at most n characters to the output iterator and returns a struct with an out field (an iterator past the last written character) and a size field (the full length that would have been produced without the limit). This is useful for writing into pre-allocated fixed-size buffers without intermediate allocations.

examples.title: Formatting ranges: vector and map
examples.description: C++23 lets you format std::vector and std::map directly via std::println. The n flag removes brackets, the double-colon syntax sets a per-element format, and std::map is printed in map style automatically.

examples.title: Specializing std::formatter for a custom type
examples.description: A std::formatter<Point> specialization makes Point formattable everywhere — in std::print, std::format, and inside larger format strings. The parse method reads the specifier; format writes via std::format_to.

examples.title: Custom format specifier and std::format_to_n
examples.description: The parse method can read flags from the specifier and change the behavior of format. std::format_to_n writes at most N characters into a buffer without an intermediate std::string.
